entities.name: manuṣya
entities.cite: for men down to the candala

entities.name: jñāna
entities.cite: already commands the pair punya and papa

axioms.name: sarvagata
axioms.cite: Everyone, acandalam — down to the outcaste, the expression marking the widest possible social range — already commands the pair punya and papa

axioms.name: alpa
axioms.cite: the treatise's prayojana there is alpa, slight

axioms.name: anapeksita
axioms.cite: Ordinary moral competence does not wait upon learning

denials.name: apeksa
denials.cite: Ordinary moral competence does not wait upon learning

claims.name: alpa
claims.cite: the need for the treatise is small

claims.name: sarvagata
claims.cite: for men down to the candala

rejected_readings.label: sastra_foundational
rejected_readings.rendering: The treatise is essential for understanding the moral distinction between merit and sin.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that sastra is necessary for basic moral knowledge, contradicting the verse's claim that all people already know punya and papa without formal instruction.

rejected_readings.label: learning_dependent_ethics
rejected_readings.rendering: Moral knowledge requires formal instruction; the common person cannot grasp the distinction between merit and sin without studying the treatise.
rejected_readings.why: Claims that jnana is dependent on formal learning (apeksa); contradicts the explicit denial established by the commentary that 'Ordinary moral competence does not wait upon learning,' which asserts moral knowledge's independence (anapeksita) from textual instruction.

notes: The verse establishes that moral knowledge (punya/papa distinction) is universal and independent of formal learning, limiting the sastra's purpose (prayojana) to refinement at the margins rather than foundational teaching.
